title: Memory: the core machinery
blurb: Encoding, storage, retrieval, capacity, duration, and the modal-model map.

steps.body:
Memory connects past information to present cognition.
The chapter defines memory as using information obtained in the past to generate a cognitive function now. That requires three linked processes: encoding creates a nervous-system representation, storage maintains it beyond immediate processing, and retrieval accesses it for a purpose. If information is never encoded, it cannot later be remembered.

steps.prompt: You hear a code, keep it available for several seconds, then call it back to type it. Which sequence best describes what happened?
choices: Retrieval → storage → encoding
Encoding → storage → retrieval
Storage → rehearsal → sensation
Attention → decay → interference
steps.reveal: The code must first be encoded, then remain stored, and finally be retrieved when you type it.

steps.body:
Memory systems are compared on two independent dimensions.
Capacity asks how much a system can hold. Duration asks how long information remains. A system can have a small capacity and brief duration, while another can hold far more for much longer. The chapter bases its claim that multiple memory systems have different properties on experiments, behavioral recordings, and observational reports.

steps.prompt: Build the Atkinson-Shiffrin sequence from first contact with a stimulus to durable storage.
steps.reveal: Sensory input → sensory memory → short-term memory → long-term memory. Attention selects sensory information for STM; encoding sends STM information to LTM; retrieval brings LTM information back into STM. This is the Atkinson-Shiffrin model, also called the modal model or information-processing model. Its timeline is roughly 1 second for sensory memory, 15–30 seconds for unrehearsed STM, and potentially lifelong storage in LTM.

steps.q: Which modal-model transition is correctly labeled?
options: Sensory memory → STM by attention
STM → sensory memory by encoding
LTM → sensory input by rehearsal
Sensory memory → LTM by retrieval
steps.explain: Attention selects part of the brief sensory trace and moves it into short-term memory.

steps.q: What distinction did the modal model make between STM and LTM?
options: STM supports current tasks; LTM supports durable storage
STM stores only visual input; LTM stores only auditory input
STM has unlimited duration; LTM lasts about one second
STM performs retrieval; LTM performs sensory transduction
steps.explain: The model separated temporary storage for current tasks from long-term storage that can persist across the lifespan.

title: The sensory-memory window
blurb: Why a rich sensory trace can be available for only an instant—and how Sperling proved it.

steps.body:
Sensory memory is a brief holding stage.
It retains information just encoded by the sensory organs before attention chooses what will enter short-term memory. The modal-model timeline places this store at roughly 1 second. Close your eyes after viewing a scene: for a moment the image seems present, then it rapidly fades.

steps.prompt: Why is sensory memory useful if it fades so quickly?
choices: It makes every sensory detail permanent
It briefly holds input for selection
It eliminates the need for short-term memory
It prevents retrieval from long-term memory
steps.reveal: Its value is the selection window: the trace holds long enough for attention to move chosen information into STM.

steps.body:
Sperling (1960) separated availability from report.
Participants briefly saw three rows of letters. In whole report, they tried to report the entire grid and produced about 25%, usually one row. In partial report, a high, medium, or low tone presented after the grid identified one row; participants reported about 75–80% of that row.

steps.prompt: The tone came after the grid disappeared, yet participants could report whichever row it selected. What does that imply?
steps.reveal: Most of the grid was briefly available in sensory memory. Whole report was low because the trace faded during the time needed to speak all the letters, not because only one row had been seen.

steps.q: What happened when Sperling increased the delay between the display and the row cue?
options: Recall increased because rehearsal improved
Recall stayed at 75–80% indefinitely
Recall fell as the sensory trace faded
Recall transferred directly into long-term memory
steps.explain: A longer display-to-tone delay left less of the rapidly fading sensory trace available.

steps.body: The exam logic: immediate partial report estimates how much was briefly available; declining performance across cue delays estimates how quickly that store disappears. Attention is the bridge from this rich but fleeting trace into STM.

title: How much fits in STM?
blurb: Auditory span, visual capacity, resource limits, and the power of meaningful chunks.

steps.body:
Short-term memory holds attended information for current use.
Without active maintenance, the deck gives a typical duration of about 15–30 seconds. Repeating a newly heard phone number keeps it active long enough to use or encode.

steps.prompt: Miller presented ordered lists of letters, numbers, or words for immediate repetition. What stable auditory span did he report?
choices: About 1 item
About 4–5 items
About 7 ± 2 items
An unlimited number
steps.reveal: Miller's classic estimate was about 7 ± 2 auditory items, with reliable individual differences.

steps.body:
Visual STM has its own limit.
In change detection, a participant sees objects such as colored squares, then judges whether a second display is identical or changed. The chapter's data suggest a capacity of roughly 4–5 visual items. A resource-pool figure also shows fewer items retained as each item becomes more complex.

steps.prompt: Why can 1776 1492 2001 2018 be easier than 1776149220012018 even though both contain the same digits?
steps.reveal: Recognizable years allow chunking: long-term knowledge turns many digits into a few meaningful wholes, reducing the number of STM units. This is why the chapter frames Miller's auditory limit as roughly 7 ± 2 chunks, not necessarily 7 ± 2 individual digits.

steps.q: What did Chase and Simon's chess result show about expert memory?
options: Experts had unlimited memory for both real and random boards
Experts led on meaningful boards, not random ones
Novices recalled meaningful positions better than experts
Random boards created larger chunks than game positions
steps.explain: Experts used learned chess configurations as chunks. Random arrangements removed the meaningful patterns that supported the advantage.

steps.q: What best explains the apparent 'super-memory' of a professional mnemonist?
options: A greatly expanded basic STM capacity
A sensory trace that never decays
They form large meaningful chunks
The absence of any long-term knowledge
steps.explain: The deck attributes exceptional string recall to chunking skill, not a fundamentally larger item limit.

title: Why short-term memories disappear
blurb: Rehearsal, the Brown-Peterson task, decay, interference, and the experiment that separated time from competition.

steps.body:
Rehearsal changes STM duration.
Rehearsal repeats information and reactivates its encoding, so information can theoretically remain active as long as rehearsal continues. To measure duration without rehearsal, the Brown-Peterson task presented a trigram and then forced participants to count backward by threes.

steps.prompt: Why did counting backward by threes matter in the Brown-Peterson task?
choices: It created meaningful chunks
It blocked rehearsal
It cued one row of letters
It moved the trigram into LTM
steps.reveal: The counting task occupied the process participants would otherwise use to repeat the trigram. Under distraction, recall suggested about a 15-second unrehearsed duration.

steps.body:
Two explanations compete.
Decay says information is lost simply as time passes. Interference says competing information displaces it. Proactive interference carries earlier information forward to disrupt later learning; retroactive interference lets newer information disrupt older material.

steps.prompt: Why did Keppel and Underwood focus on the first few trials of the Peterson task?
steps.reveal: Early trials contain little buildup from prior trigrams. Recall stayed fairly high for up to about 15 seconds there, showing that proactive interference contributed to the later forgetting curve. Considering later trials left room for both interference and decay.

steps.q: Lewandowsky et al. blocked rehearsal with articulatory suppression and varied recall speed. Recall did not worsen at slower speeds. What follows?
options: Time alone caused no decay without interference
Slower recall always strengthens sensory memory
Articulatory suppression creates long-term storage
Only retroactive interference can affect letters
steps.explain: If elapsed time alone caused forgetting, slower output should have reduced recall. It did not, so the study argued against pure time-based decay.

steps.body:
Keep the studies straight.
Peterson's curve first looked like decay. Keppel and Underwood showed that prior trials created proactive interference. Lewandowsky isolated the passage of time more carefully and found no speed effect. The chapter therefore treats the decay-versus-interference question as an empirical design problem, not a definition to memorize in isolation.

title: Working memory as an active system
blurb: Specialized stores, executive filtering, the episodic buffer, individual differences, and training claims.

steps.body:
Baddeley replaced a passive box with an active workspace.
The visuo-spatial sketchpad holds and manipulates visual information. The phonological loop repeats and analyzes auditory information. The central executive coordinates the two stores and controls what enters working memory.

steps.prompt: Match each task to its best component: mentally rotate a shape; silently repeat a sentence; switch attention between the two.
steps.reveal: Mental rotation → visuo-spatial sketchpad. Repeating the sentence → phonological loop. Coordinating and switching → central executive.

steps.body:
Brooks (1968) tested separate memory codes.
Participants completed verbal or visual-memory tasks using either spoken yes/no responses or pointing on a visual grid. The visual diagram task slowed dramatically with the visual pointing response, supporting distinct auditory/verbal and visual buffers with modality-specific competition.

steps.q: Which Brooks result most strongly supports a separate visual working-memory code?
options: The visual task slowed most with visual pointing
Every task was equally fast with every response mode
The verbal task could not be completed with speech
Participants remembered random chess boards perfectly
steps.explain: The large within-modality cost is what a separate, capacity-limited visual code predicts.

steps.body:
The central executive protects limited space.
In Vogel et al. (2005), high- and low-capacity participants remembered blue rectangles while red rectangles sometimes acted as distractors. With targets alone, both groups' ERP activity tracked two versus four items. With distractors, low-capacity participants showed a strong load response, while high-capacity participants showed a weaker response—evidence that filtering helps determine apparent capacity. The deck applies the same idea to some age-related decline. Its graphs show processing speed, working memory, and LTM declining while world knowledge is more preserved; an adjacent visual shows lower total gray-matter volume and less elaborate neural structure with age. High literacy or numeracy use tracks better skill maintenance than low use in the displayed age-skill profiles.

steps.prompt: Why was an episodic buffer added if the visual and auditory stores are separate?
choices: To erase long-term memories during a task
To bind visual, auditory, and LTM into an episode
To replace the central executive with sensory memory
To store only speech without a time limit
steps.reveal: The episodic buffer is a time-limited store controlled by the central executive. It binds the specialized stores with long-term knowledge into a multisensory, time-based representation.

steps.q: Which statement best captures the chapter's conclusion about individual differences and brain training?
options: Working-memory capacity is unrelated to reasoning or mathematics
Training never improves performance on working-memory tasks
WM correlates with g; broad training transfer is unclear
Any commercial app has proven permanent gains across all cognition
steps.explain: Working-memory capacity correlates with g and skills such as math and reasoning. The deck cites musical training alongside improved WM in young children, and WM practice can improve trained performance, but wide transfer claims remain controversial.

title: The neural evidence for working memory
blurb: Distributed modality networks, sustained prefrontal activity, human imaging, and lesion evidence.

steps.body:
Working memory is distributed.
It begins with perceptual encoding and continues while information is actively maintained. Cabeza and Nyberg's meta-analysis found no single working-memory region. Auditory tasks recruit auditory areas such as temporal cortex; visual tasks recruit visual areas such as occipital cortex. Frontal regions are especially important for executive demands.

steps.prompt: Why can the chapter say both 'working memory happens all over the brain' and 'the frontal lobe is especially important'?
steps.reveal: Modality-specific regions represent the content, while frontal regions contribute strongly to active maintenance and executive control. A distributed system can still have a particularly important control region.

steps.body:
Funahashi et al. (1989) recorded prefrontal neurons.
A neuron that responded to a square's location fired strongly during the delay when a monkey had to remember that location. The sustained delay response disappeared when no memory task was required, tying the activity to active maintenance rather than the stimulus alone.

steps.q: What human result from Schon et al. (2008) paralleled Funahashi's delay activity?
options: Prefrontal activity rose during the memory delay
Temporal activity vanished while participants listened to speech
Occipital damage improved delayed matching
A cue tone increased sensory-memory duration
steps.explain: In a delayed-match-to-sample task, human prefrontal activity increased during the period when the first image had to be maintained.

steps.body:
Activation shows association; damage tests necessity more directly.
Moore et al. (2010) found that damage to dorsolateral prefrontal cortex impaired tasks similar to delayed matching. Because frontal lobes support executive function, the deck proposes that this region may contribute to the central executive.

steps.q: Why is the Moore lesion finding stronger causal evidence than activation alone?
options: Damage impaired working-memory performance
The region became active during every sensory event
The study proved all memory is stored in one neuron
The task removed every executive demand
steps.explain: When disruption of a region accompanies loss of function, the evidence more directly supports a necessary contribution than a correlation from activity alone.

title: Memory systems through amnesia
blurb: Use H.M., Clive Wearing, and K.F. to separate short-term and long-term memory.

steps.body:
STM and LTM differ in more than duration.
Short-term memory briefly retains specific stimulus details, usually for about 15–30 seconds. Long-term memory can retain abstracted semantic information indefinitely, but stored information must remain retrievable.

Put amnesia on a timeline.
Retrograde amnesia removes access to events before a neurological event. Anterograde amnesia impairs memory for new information encountered afterward. A person can show either pattern or both.

steps.prompt: A patient remembers childhood but repeatedly forgets conversations held after an injury. Which pattern is most direct?
choices: Retrograde amnesia
Anterograde amnesia
A primacy effect
A procedural-memory advantage
steps.reveal: The missing information was encountered after the event, so this is anterograde amnesia.

steps.body:
H.M. and Clive Wearing preserved islands of function.
After hippocampal surgery, H.M. retained identity, general knowledge, and immediate memory but could not form new LTM. Wearing had severe retrograde and anterograde amnesia yet retained brief STM, language, piano skill, and basic facts.

steps.prompt: Why is Patient K.F.—new LTM formation with an STM span of only two or three items—especially valuable evidence?
choices: K.F. exactly duplicates H.M.'s deficit
K.F. shows that rehearsal is unnecessary
K.F. supplies the reverse STM-LTM pattern
K.F. proves all memory is hippocampal
steps.reveal: H.M. preserved STM but lost new LTM; K.F. impaired STM but preserved new LTM. The crossed pattern supports distinct systems.

steps.q: Which observation most strongly argues that STM and LTM rely on distinguishable mechanisms?
options: Every patient forgets both recent and remote events equally
H.M. and K.F. show opposite preservation-and-impairment patterns
The final items in every list are recalled better than the middle
Category cues improve recall compared with an unguided search
steps.explain: Opposing dissociations show that one memory system can function when the other is impaired.

title: Encoding succeeds only when retrieval works
blurb: Connect rehearsal, serial position, processing depth, transfer, and encoding specificity.

steps.body:
Rehearsal is not all equal.
Maintenance rehearsal repeats information without added thought. Elaborative rehearsal considers meaning and is more effective at moving information toward LTM.

The serial-position curve reveals two mechanisms.
Early items benefit from rehearsal and LTM—the primacy effect. Final items remain active in STM—the recency effect. A 30-second delay removes recency.

steps.prompt: A delay after the last word erases the end-of-list advantage. What does that imply?
choices: Recency normally depends on STM
Primacy normally depends on STM
Every list item was semantically encoded
Category cues caused the original curve
steps.reveal: The final items lose their advantage when they are no longer active in STM.

steps.body:
Depth helps—but match can win.
Craik and Tulving found best memory after meaning-based sentence completion. Morris showed that rhyme encoding can win when the test cue also asks for rhyme. Transfer-appropriate processing is the match between encoding and retrieval operations.

Cues recover what an unguided search may miss.
Tulving and Pearlstone improved recall with category cues. Encoding specificity extends this: cues linked to the original episode—including incidental surroundings—can facilitate retrieval.

steps.prompt: Godden and Baddeley's divers learn words underwater. Which test condition should provide the strongest contextual support?
choices: Recall on land after changing mood
Recall underwater in the matching environment
Recall anywhere after a 30-second delay
Recall only the final three words
steps.reveal: Context-dependent memory improves when the external environment matches at encoding and retrieval.

steps.q: Which principle best explains why a rhyme cue can favor rhyme-based study over deeper sentence study?
options: The spacing effect
The primacy effect
Transfer-appropriate processing
System consolidation
steps.explain: Memory depends on how well the retrieval operation matches the operation used at encoding.

title: Space it, then retrieve it
blurb: Turn the spacing and testing effects into an exam-ready study strategy.

steps.body:
Spacing beats cramming.
Recall is higher when repetitions occur across separated study periods. Cepeda and colleagues suggest reviewing after roughly 10–20% of the interval before final retrieval; for a test in one week, the slide uses about a one-day gap.

steps.prompt: Your test is one week away. Which schedule follows the chapter's spacing example?
choices: One long session the night before
One session now and no later contact
Several reviews separated by about a day
Continuous rereading without a break
steps.reveal: A one-day gap is roughly 10–20% of a week and distributes practice across multiple sessions.

steps.body:
Testing is learning.
The testing effect means that producing information from memory strengthens later retention more than passively seeing it again. Answer first; then check and correct.

The paired-associate table isolates retrieval practice.
Full study/full test scored 81%. Reduced repeat study plus full repeat testing also scored 81%. Full repeat study plus reduced repeat testing fell to 36%. Re-exposure did not replace retrieval.

steps.prompt: Why did the less-study group still match the full-study group at 81%?
choices: It kept testing every pair
It never saw missed pairs again
It removed all spacing between sessions
It used only context-dependent cues
steps.reveal: Comprehensive repeated retrieval remained even though repeat study was limited to missed pairs.

steps.q: Which plan combines both chapter-supported retention factors?
options: Reread continuously during one final-night session
Use spaced sessions and retrieve before checking answers
Keep every answer visible while copying it repeatedly
Avoid testing until the real exam to protect the memory
steps.explain: Distributed sessions create spacing, and closed-book production creates retrieval practice.

title: Two ways to declare the past
blurb: Distinguish episodic events from semantic knowledge behaviorally and neurally.

steps.body:
Explicit means reportable.
Declarative memories can be consciously described. Episodic memory represents personally experienced events with ordered sensory detail; semantic memory represents facts and meanings without that event context.

steps.prompt: Which is semantic rather than episodic?
choices: Reliving a birthday dinner
Remembering the room where class met
Knowing your current address
Replaying the order of a personal event
steps.reveal: An address is reportable factual knowledge without the sensory sequence of a personal episode.

steps.body:
Episodes can lose their context.
You may retain course content while forgetting what you wore or where the professor stood. The original episodic memory has become more semantic as event details fade.

Patient dissociations separate the systems.
K.C. had no episodic memory but retained some semantic memory and could learn facts about his life. A contrasting encephalitis patient preserved old and new episodes while losing word meanings and famous identities.

steps.prompt: What makes the two patient cases stronger together?
choices: They show identical deficits after identical injuries
They show opposite episodic-semantic patterns
They show that both systems are procedural
They show that neither system can learn
steps.reveal: Each declarative system can be disproportionately impaired while the other remains functional.

steps.q: What did Levine and colleagues' fMRI comparison suggest about episodic retrieval?
options: It reactivates occipital-temporal sensory regions engaged during encoding
It relies only on frontal-parietal abstraction with no sensory component
It produces exactly the same activation as basic semantic facts
It is stored only as a short-term trace that disappears within 30 seconds
steps.explain: Personal-event recordings engaged occipital and temporal regions, consistent with reactivating encoding-related sensory areas.

title: Memory without remembering
blurb: See how skills, bias, familiarity, conditioning, and mirror drawing reveal implicit memory.

steps.body:
Implicit memory operates outside awareness.
Stored information can guide behavior without conscious retrieval. Procedural memories—walking, swimming, bicycling—are automatic learned action patterns and are relatively resistant to forgetting.

The IAT measures compatibility through speed.
People classify items under two category pairings. Faster responses for one shared-key pairing than the reversed pairing are interpreted as a stronger automatic association.

steps.prompt: STEM + Man is sorted faster than STEM + Woman. What does the chapter's example infer?
choices: The faster pairing reflects a stronger automatic association
The slower pairing proves lower real-world ability
Both pairings reveal only consciously declared beliefs
The task measures serial-position memory
steps.reveal: The IAT interprets response-time compatibility as an implicit association, not as a measure of actual ability.

steps.body:
Prior exposure changes judgment.
The familiarity effect raises liking for previously encountered stimuli without recognition. The propaganda effect raises perceived truth for previously heard statements regardless of accuracy.

Conditioning stores durable associations.
Operant conditioning links stimuli with voluntary responses; classical conditioning links stimuli with involuntary responses. Patient S.M.'s very low fear after bilateral amygdala damage supports an amygdala role in fear conditioning.

H.M. learned without remembering learning.
Across mirror-drawing sessions he improved a new mapping skill, although he could not remember the training episodes. Procedural learning survived profound explicit-memory impairment.

steps.q: Which result most cleanly demonstrates durable implicit procedural learning?
options: A student verbally recalls where a fact was learned
H.M. improves at mirror drawing without remembering practice
A diver recalls more in the same external setting
A participant remembers the first and last list items
steps.explain: Performance improved across sessions even though conscious memory for the learning episodes was absent.

title: How long-term memories stabilize
blurb: Link synaptic strengthening, hippocampal-cortical consolidation, replay, and transfer learning.

steps.body:
Consolidation makes memory durable.
Synaptic consolidation occurs at connections between neurons. With long-term potentiation, repeated stimulation increases effective transmission so the receiving neuron becomes more likely to fire.

System consolidation reorganizes storage.
It lays down cortical connections through hippocampal-cortical cooperation. It is much slower than synaptic consolidation and can continue across a lifetime.

The hippocampus has two proposed roles.
It may initially store memories and later move them to cortex, or it may coordinate storage that occurs in cortex. The exact role remains debated in the chapter.

steps.prompt: A diagram shows cortical links strengthening while hippocampal links fade across time. What does it depict?
choices: System consolidation
The testing effect
State-dependent memory
The recency effect
steps.reveal: The memory becomes increasingly supported by cortical connections as system consolidation progresses.

steps.body:
Replay repeats the ordered neural sequence.
After encoding, hippocampal activity can replay in the same temporal order but much faster. The place-cell figure compresses an active sequence of about six seconds into roughly two-tenths of a second during sleep.

Stability explains the retrograde gradient.
Events just before a car accident may be forgotten because they were still consolidating, while older memories had more time to stabilize. The chapter closes with an ANN analogy: transfer learning lets an older network help train a new one on a task that shares information.

steps.q: Which statement correctly contrasts the two consolidation mechanisms?
options: Synaptic consolidation strengthens local connections; system consolidation reorganizes hippocampal-cortical support over time.
Synaptic consolidation occurs only in STM; system consolidation occurs only during an IAT.
Synaptic consolidation requires context cues; system consolidation requires rhyme cues.
Synaptic consolidation is slower than a lifetime; system consolidation ends within 30 seconds.
steps.explain: The chapter separates local synaptic strengthening from slower large-scale stabilization across hippocampus and cortex.

title: How Your Life Becomes a Memory System
blurb: Build autobiographical memory from its functions, working self, and three levels of detail.

steps.body: Autobiographical memory combines episodic memories of events you experienced with semantic facts about yourself. The speaker notes describe retrieval as multidimensional mental time travel with spatial, emotional, and sensory components. Researchers rely on retrospective interviews, limiting control over encoding order and making accuracy difficult to verify.

steps.prompt: You remember a failed study strategy and change your plan for the next exam. Which function is doing the work?
choices: Social
Directive
Self-representational
Infantile
steps.reveal: The directive function uses a remembered event to guide a current action. Social memory builds bonds; self-representational memory supports identity.

steps.body: Autobiographical memory also has a social function—shared experiences create bonds—and a self-representational function—the past supports a stable self-image. One memory can serve more than one function, so identify what the example emphasizes.

steps.prompt: In the self-memory system, why might scholarship-related memories become easier to retrieve while you write an application?
choices: The working self uses the current goal to access the knowledge base
The knowledge base deletes unrelated events
Every episodic detail becomes semantic
Lifetime periods are stored outside autobiographical memory
steps.reveal: The working self is the active set of current goals. It controls access to the autobiographical knowledge base, the colder store of past-life information.

steps.body: Autobiographical knowledge is organized from lifetime periods to general events to event-specific knowledge. Most detailed episodes last only hours or days; their event-specific detail may dissolve, leaving a broader semantic fact or no accessible memory.

steps.q: Years later, you know you lived in Madrid but cannot re-experience most individual days. What has most likely happened in the SMS framework?
options: Event-specific knowledge faded while broader self-knowledge remained
The working self converted the experience into a motor skill
Every daily episode remained fully preserved
The lifetime period was never encoded
steps.explain: The broad lifetime-period fact can remain after event-specific sensory and contextual knowledge dissolves.

title: The Blank Beginning and the Reminiscence Bump
blurb: Explain early-childhood forgetting and why life transitions become unusually memorable.

steps.body: Infantile amnesia is the adult scarcity of episodic memories from roughly before ages two to four. A less severe form can affect memories from before age ten, and explicit memory is affected more than implicit memory; young children can still learn.

steps.prompt: Which proposed explanation says that building the developing brain may overwrite early traces?
choices: Language ability
State-dependent learning
Self-image formation
Neurogenesis
steps.reveal: The neurogenesis account proposes that adding new neurons during development can overwrite earlier traces.

steps.body: The chapter offers several compatible accounts: the hippocampus is still developing until about age four; neurogenesis may overwrite traces; immature language limits later story-like reconstruction; and a child's internal emotional state may not match the adult retrieval state. Bauer and Larkina found the fastest forgetting in four-year-olds.

steps.q: Which finding came from Bauer and Larkina's longitudinal work?
options: Four-year-olds showed the quickest forgetting
Ten-year-olds had no episodic memory
Implicit memory disappeared before explicit memory
Adults recovered every memory after receiving a cue
steps.explain: Their longitudinal data showed especially rapid forgetting in the four-year-old group.

steps.body: The reminiscence bump is enhanced recall for late adolescence and early adulthood. The self-image hypothesis links it to identity formation. The cognitive hypothesis links it to rapid change followed by stability.

steps.prompt: People who emigrated at ages 34–35 show a later bump than people who emigrated at 20–24. Which account does that shift favor?
choices: A fixed biological bump at the same age for everyone
The cognitive hypothesis based on life transition
Infantile amnesia caused by hippocampal immaturity
The misinformation effect caused by leading questions
steps.reveal: The bump moved with the timing of a major transition, supporting the cognitive hypothesis rather than a fixed-age explanation.

title: Why Emotional Memories Feel Permanent—but Aren't
blurb: Connect attention and the amygdala to emotional encoding, flashbulb accuracy, and confidence.

steps.body: Emotion can improve memory because it captures attention and strengthens encoding. In an attentional blink, a second rapid target is usually missed, yet Anderson found that emotionally charged words were more likely than neutral words to break through.

steps.prompt: After 'chair,' participants rapidly see either 'ball' or 'murder.' Which second word should be detected more often?
choices: Ball, because neutral words avoid the blink
Both equally, because presentation speed controls everything
Neither, because all second targets are invisible
Murder, because emotional salience draws processing
steps.reveal: The emotionally charged word murder received a perceptual advantage, particularly consistent with the chapter's emphasis on negative valence.

steps.body: The amygdala supports emotional encoding. Bilateral damage removed the emotional-blink advantage; in this word task, left—but not right—hemisphere damage also removed it. Temporal-lobe damage involving the amygdala weakened emotional-event recall more than frontal-lobe damage.

steps.q: Which lesion group should show the weakest recall for an emotional public event?
options: Healthy controls
Frontal-lobe damage with intact amygdala
Temporal-lobe damage involving the amygdala
High frontal-lobe functioning
steps.explain: The chapter's lesion comparison tied the weakest emotional-event recall to temporal-lobe damage involving the amygdala.

steps.body: Flashbulb memories are vivid memories for shared, high-emotion events. They are not permanent recordings: Neisser and Harsch found changed reports with high confidence, and Talarico and Rubin found declining accuracy while flashbulb confidence stayed high. Relevance and identity also matter, as Brown and Kulik's cultural comparison showed.

steps.prompt: A person's account of a public tragedy changes over two years, but confidence stays extremely high. What is the best conclusion?
choices: High confidence proves the later account is accurate
Everyday memories are always more vivid than public-event memories
The event cannot qualify as a flashbulb memory
Flashbulb memories can remain confident while their details change
steps.reveal: Flashbulb memories can preserve confidence and vividness even while accuracy declines. Confidence is not a substitute for consistency.

title: How the Mind Builds a Memory That Never Happened
blurb: Use semantic lures, leading questions, and suggestion to understand false details and false events.

steps.body: Autobiographical memory is an unconscious construction, not a recording. The mind interprets an event through existing beliefs and semantic organization, so a sincere memory can still be false.

steps.prompt: You study 'bed, rest, pillow, slumber, awake' but never see 'sleep.' Why might 'sleep' appear at recall?
choices: It was the first word and received a primacy boost
The semantic theme activated an unpresented critical lure
It was encoded as a procedural action
The word was supplied during a lineup
steps.reveal: The slide labels the Deese-Roediger-McDermott paradigm 'RDM,' but the standard abbreviation is DRM. Related words activate the unpresented critical lure; about 40% reported 'sleep.'

steps.body: The misinformation effect occurs when incorrect post-event information becomes part of memory. Loftus and Palmer showed seven crash videos in random order and changed the verb in a speed question: more forceful wording produced higher estimates and more reports of broken glass that was never shown.

steps.q: Which wording should create the most reports of nonexistent broken glass?
options: How fast were the cars going?
How fast were the cars going when they hit?
How fast were the cars going when they smashed?
Did the cars stop before making contact?
steps.explain: The most forceful verb, 'smashed,' produced the most false reports of broken glass.

steps.body: Suggestion can do more than alter one detail. Loftus and Pickrell supplied four childhood stories, including one false lost-in-public event. About 25% accepted and elaborated the false event; most still rejected it.

steps.prompt: What is the crucial difference between the traffic-film studies and the lost-in-public study?
choices: Only the traffic studies used autobiographical memory
The lost story tested motor learning rather than remembering
Everyone accepted the lost story, but no one accepted broken glass
The traffic studies altered details; the lost story could create a whole event
steps.reveal: The traffic questions reshaped details of a witnessed event; the lost-in-public suggestion could generate an entire event that never occurred.

title: Familiar Face, Wrong Source, Reopened Memory
blurb: Master source-monitoring errors and the vulnerable window created by retrieval.

steps.body: A source-monitoring error happens when information is familiar but the encounter that produced the familiarity is misremembered. Because later information alters an older memory, false memory can function as retroactive interference.

steps.prompt: A victim recognizes a suspect, but his strong alibi shows she had seen him earlier on television. What failed?
choices: Recognition of the face
Source monitoring for where the face was encountered
Implicit motor learning
The directive function of memory
steps.reveal: The face was genuinely familiar; the error was assigning that familiarity to the assault rather than the television appearance.

steps.body: Ross and colleagues created that problem experimentally. Participants who first saw a male observer beside a teacher later tended to select that innocent man from a robbery lineup. The earlier video supplied the familiarity; the robbery supplied the wrong source.

steps.q: Which Ross-study group should be most likely to identify the innocent observer as the robber?
options: The group that first saw the male observer with the teacher
The group that first saw only the teacher
A group that never saw the robbery clip
A group that completed no lineup
steps.explain: Only the observer-present group had a competing, innocent source of familiarity with that male face.

steps.body: Reconsolidation occurs when an accessed memory is stored again. Chan found that recalling a television episode before misinformation increased later false recall. In Schiller's study, the ten-minute reminder condition showed no spontaneous recovery of fear, whereas the no-reminder condition showed fear when tested again.

steps.prompt: On the Schiller result slide, which condition is explicitly shown as preventing spontaneous recovery of fear to the blue circle?
choices: No reminder before extinction
A reminder six hours before extinction
A reminder ten minutes before extinction
Shock trials with no extinction
steps.reveal: The ten-minute reminder likely reopened the fear memory while extinction could update it. The deck shows recovered fear in the no-reminder condition but does not state the six-hour condition's outcome.

title: Safer Testimony and the Brain Network Behind the Story
blurb: Turn memory science into better eyewitness procedure, then map autobiographical recall across the brain.

steps.body: Eyewitness testimony is powerful because confidence persuades juries, but constructive memory means confidence can accompany error. The slide says the Innocence Project used DNA to overturn 350 eyewitness-based convictions since 1982 and estimated 100,000–200,000 wrongful convictions from eyewitness testimony.

steps.prompt: Which opening is least likely to contaminate an eyewitness account?
choices: The robber wore red, correct?
Another witness chose number three—do you agree?
Tell me everything you remember in your own words
Choose the closest match because the robber is in this lineup
steps.reveal: Begin with free recall. The other openings insert information or pressure the witness to choose.

steps.body: The chapter recommends written statements in neutral language and lineups that show one image at a time. Witnesses should be told that the assailant may not be present. The shared goal is to reduce suggestion, emotional interference, and forced choice.

steps.q: Which lineup instruction best follows the chapter?
options: One of these people is definitely the assailant
The assailant may be absent; consider each image separately
Pick the person who looks most like the others
Use the investigator's preferred choice if uncertain
steps.explain: The chapter specifically recommends sequential viewing plus a warning that the assailant may not be present.

steps.body: Autobiographical memory depends on a distributed network: especially left prefrontal cortex reconstructs multisensory information, the medial temporal lobe and hippocampus support retrieval, and the amygdala contributes to emotional encoding and retrieval. The chapter's AI summary adds that deep Q-learning uses replay memory to make past experience useful later.

steps.q: A patient remembers that an event occurred but struggles to rebuild its combined sensory and spatial context. Which region is most directly tied to that reconstruction?
options: Amygdala
Hippocampus
Left prefrontal cortex
Primary motor cortex
steps.explain: The chapter assigns multisensory reconstruction to the prefrontal cortex, particularly the left side.

title: How knowledge fills in the world
blurb: Semantic inference, commonsense knowledge, categories, concepts, and the classical view.

steps.body:
Knowledge does more than store facts.
When you read that Ayesha dropped a balloon, you infer she is probably a child, the balloon rose, and her nose is in the middle of her face. The sentence never states those facts; interconnected semantic knowledge supplies them.

steps.prompt: You immediately know where Ayesha's nose is, but you cannot remember ever being taught that fact. What does that reveal?
choices: Much commonsense knowledge is implicit
Knowledge must always be episodic
The inference comes from sensory memory
Knowledge is stored as isolated facts
steps.reveal: Implicit commonsense knowledge can guide interpretation without conscious rule retrieval. Humans do this effortlessly; computers struggle with it.

steps.body:
Keep the three levels straight.
A category is a set of related items. An exemplar is one individual category member. A concept is the mental representation used to think about the items.

The classical view
A category is defined by features that are necessary (membership is impossible without them) and jointly sufficient (having them guarantees membership). This produces a sharp in-or-out boundary.

steps.prompt: Why does a three-legged, barkless dog cause trouble for a simple classical definition based on four legs and barking?
choices: The dog is really a bird
It proves semantic memory is temporary
It remains a dog despite lacking proposed defining features
It shows all natural-category members are equally typical once membership has been established
steps.reveal: Natural categories often lack a tidy feature list that is both necessary and sufficient. Membership survives exceptions.

steps.q: A specific robin seen outside the library is a(n) ___; the mental representation of birds is a(n) ___.
options: category; property
concept; exemplar
prototype; category
exemplar; concept
steps.explain: The robin is one individual exemplar. The concept is the mental representation used to think about birds.

title: Why a robin feels birdier
blurb: Typicality ratings, processing advantages, lexical priming, and the classical-view problem.

steps.body:
Membership is not the same as representativeness.
A robin and an ostrich are both birds, but people rate a robin as a better example. This graded treatment of valid members is a typicality effect.

Rosch (1975)
Participants rated how good an example each item was of a category and showed high agreement. Typical members are also named first and categorized faster. These effects appear early in development.

steps.prompt: What would you predict in a rapid category task?
choices: Ostrich will always be rejected as a bird despite being a legitimate category member
Typicality will affect only adults
Every bird will produce identical reaction time
Robin will usually be classified as a bird faster than ostrich
steps.reveal: Typical items receive a processing advantage even when atypical items are still legitimate members.

steps.body:
Lexical priming
In a lexical decision task, people decide whether a letter string is a word. A related prime speeds recognition of the target, and the effect is especially robust for typical words.

Typicality reaches language and memory.
Onishi et al. found that when a sentence paired an atypical and typical member, participants were more likely to reproduce the typical word. Typicality is not merely a rating preference.

steps.q: Why is graded typicality a problem for a strict necessary-and-sufficient classical theory?
options: A strict rule predicts all valid members should have equal membership status
The theory predicts no category can have members
The theory requires every category member to be stored as a separate detailed exemplar
Typicality occurs only when a rule is absent
steps.explain: An all-or-none rule explains membership but not why one valid member feels more representative or is processed faster than another.

title: Prototype versus exemplar
blurb: Two similarity-based theories, their signature predictions, and the feature-selection problem.

steps.body:
Prototype theory = compare with an average.
A prototype combines characteristic features that are likely but not required. It is an abstract category average and may not match any real member exactly.

steps.prompt: A rainbow lorikeet is prototypical in Australia but not in the United States. What does this show?
choices: Prototypes are fixed collections of necessary features shared across every cultural context
Prototype content depends on context and experience
Exemplars cannot be remembered
Category membership changes with location
steps.reveal: The abstract average reflects the examples common in a person's environment. Prototype similarity is contextual.

steps.body:
Exemplar theory = compare with stored instances.
Instead of keeping only an average, memory retains actual category members. A new item is classified according to similarity to those encountered examples.

Dopkins & Gleason's diagnostic test
An ambiguous rectangle matched Category 2's prototype but was closest to one Category 1 exemplar. Participants chose Category 1 more often — the exemplar prediction.

steps.prompt: Which theory best explains recognizing a rare bird because it resembles one unusual bird you saw years ago?
choices: Classical theory
Prototype theory only
Exemplar theory
Cognitive economy
steps.reveal: A specific stored member can rescue an item that is far from the category prototype.

steps.q: Murphy and Medin's 'infinite similarities' criticism says prototype and exemplar theories need to explain:
options: Why natural categories can contain members unless every possible similarity is stored
Why memory has a limited duration
How a prototype becomes an essence
Which possible similarities are relevant for a categorization
steps.explain: Almost any two objects share countless trivial features. A complete theory needs knowledge to select the similarities that matter.

title: Categories need explanations
blurb: Knowledge-based coherence, psychological essentialism, and the path from efficiency to stereotyping.

steps.body:
Similarity is not enough.
Knowledge-based theories say broad background knowledge explains why features belong together and tells the mind which comparisons are meaningful.

Coherence advantage
Murphy & Allopenna found that an underwater building with divers, thick windows, and submarine access was easier to learn than an equally long but incoherent desert/farmer/submarine description. Existing knowledge connects the first set.

steps.prompt: Why is the coherent underwater description easier to learn?
choices: It has a complete necessary-and-sufficient definition that applies to every possible building
Its features are causally and thematically related by prior knowledge
It contains no semantic information
It exactly matches one stored rectangle
steps.reveal: Knowledge supplies a reason for the features to co-occur. Coherence supports category learning.

steps.body:
Psychological essentialism
People often assume category members share an underlying essence that determines membership. This can organize biological kinds, but the assumed essence may be invisible or unknown.

The social danger
Applying essentialism to people encourages the false idea that every group member shares fixed traits. Bastian & Haslam linked stronger essentialist beliefs with more stereotyping.

steps.q: Why does the mind keep categorizing despite this social risk?
options: It reduces cognitive load when navigating a complex world
It makes every inference about each individual category member accurate and exception-free
It prevents all individual differences
It eliminates the need for semantic memory
steps.explain: Categories make cognition efficient, but efficiency can become overgeneralization. Use the shortcut without treating it as certainty about individuals.

title: How concepts connect
blurb: Category levels, cognitive economy, property inheritance, verification time, and spreading activation.

steps.body:
Three category levels
The basic level is 'just right' — informative and distinctive. A subordinate level adds detail but shares much with nearby members. A superordinate level is broad and distinctive but less informative. In the bird-picture graph, nonexperts favored the basic label 'bird,' while experts more often used specific labels such as 'sparrow' or 'robin.'

steps.prompt: Which level gives the best everyday balance of useful detail and distinctiveness?
choices: Superordinate
Basic
Subordinate
Exemplar
steps.reveal: Rosch called the basic level special because it balances information with distinction.

steps.body:
Collins–Quillian hierarchy
Concepts are nodes linked to properties and other nodes. Cognitive economy stores a shared property at the highest appropriate level; property inheritance lets lower nodes access it.

Distance predicts reaction time.
The visual evidence figure shows slower verification as both category and property judgments cross more hierarchy levels. The model therefore predicts that 'a canary is an animal' takes longer than 'a canary is a bird.' Some results — such as pig–animal being faster than pig–mammal — violate this simple rule.

Collins–Loftus spreading activation
The revised network drops a fixed hierarchy. Concepts are connected by semantic relatedness, and activation spreads quickly among close meanings — explaining lexical priming and typicality.

steps.q: What is the major structural difference between spreading activation and the original hierarchical network?
options: Spreading activation stores no concepts
It uses necessary features only
It organizes concepts by semantic relatedness instead of fixed levels
It cannot predict priming
steps.explain: Semantic closeness, not a rigid tree, determines how activation spreads in the later model.

title: Knowledge in memory and the body
blurb: Schemas, reconstructive memory, repeated reproduction, and grounded cognition.

steps.body:
A schema is an organized knowledge base about a topic.
Schemas make interpretation fast by supplying expected structure — but when exact detail is weak, the same expectations can fill gaps incorrectly.

Bartlett's repeated reproduction
Participants repeatedly drew an image from memory across days. Exact features disappeared while the image became more face-like, showing that memory is reconstructed using schematic knowledge.

steps.prompt: A witness inserts a bookshelf into a remembered office because it fits the office schema. What happened?
choices: A schema reconstructed a plausible detail that was never seen
Property inheritance produced exact recall
The witness accessed an ATL hub without spokes
A prototype guaranteed the detail was present
steps.reveal: Schemas support useful reconstruction, but plausible expectations can be mistaken for observed details.

steps.body:
Embodied cognition
The body influences thought. Matching a reclined posture improved retrieval of a past dental visit, and holding hot coffee shifted judgments of another person's 'warmth.' The speaker note adds that favorable parole decisions were about 65% just after judges ate but nearly 0% just before a meal break.

Action meaning recruits action systems.
Hauk and colleagues found related motor-region activity when people moved body parts and when they silently read matching action words such as lick, pick, and kick.

The symbol-grounding limit
The chapter summary notes that artificial neural networks can produce useful knowledge outputs from assigned symbols, but those symbols are not grounded in direct experience unless the system connects to the world through something such as robotics.

steps.q: Which finding most directly supports a grounded representation of action words?
options: All category members receive equal ratings
Semantic dementia follows ATL degeneration
A canary inherits bird properties
Reading 'kick' recruits activity related to foot movement
steps.explain: Overlap between action execution and action-word processing links meaning to sensorimotor systems.

title: Where semantic knowledge lives
blurb: Semantic dementia, the anterior temporal hub, distributed spokes, and virtual-lesion evidence.

steps.body:
Selective semantic loss
Warrington described patients who could see and speak but could not name pictured objects or state their properties. Semantic dementia progressively removes knowledge while other abilities can remain relatively intact.

The localization puzzle
Semantic dementia is strongly associated with anterior temporal lobe (ATL) degeneration, yet healthy semantic tasks often activate widely distributed cortex. Neither a single-location nor a fully diffuse story is enough.

steps.prompt: Which model was designed to reconcile localized ATL patient evidence with distributed neuroimaging activity?
choices: Classical categorization
Hub-and-spoke model
Modal memory model
Brown–Peterson model
steps.reveal: The hub-and-spoke model combines a specialized integration hub with distributed feature systems.

steps.body:
Hub versus spokes
The ATL hub integrates generalized, abstract meaning. Distributed cortical spokes preserve context-dependent and modality-specific details such as appearance, sound, and action.

TMS tests causality.
Transcranial magnetic stimulation temporarily disrupts a localized region — a 'virtual lesion.' Pobric and colleagues' ATL/IPL results supported both localized and distributed contributions to knowledge.

steps.q: In the hub-and-spoke model, where is generalized cross-modal meaning represented?
options: Only in the visual spoke
Only in the inferior parietal spoke
In the anterior temporal hub
In short-term sensory memory
steps.explain: The ATL hub integrates feature information across the distributed spokes into an abstract semantic representation.
